title: Subordinate Clauses — weil, dass, wenn
description: Join ideas like a native: because, that, when/if. These conjunctions do something remarkable — they kick the verb to the very end of their clause. It's the final big word-order rule of A2.

lessons.title: weil & dass — Verb to the End
lessons.subtitle: 'because' and 'that' send the conjugated verb to the last position.
lessons.hook: 'I'm learning German because I want a job in Germany.' In English the verb stays put. In German, 'weil' (because) does something wild: it throws the conjugated verb all the way to the end. 'Ich lerne Deutsch, weil ich einen Job in Deutschland will.' Weird once, natural forever.
lessons.explain: Subordinating conjunctions like weil (because) and dass (that) start a subordinate clause, and in that clause the conjugated verb moves to the FINAL position. There's always a comma before the conjunction. Compare: main clause 'Ich will einen Job' (verb second) becomes '…, weil ich einen Job will' (verb last). With dass: 'Ich denke, dass Deutsch wichtig ist' (I think that German is important). If there are two verbs, the conjugated one still goes last: '…, weil ich arbeiten muss.'

examples.german: Ich lerne Deutsch, weil ich in Deutschland arbeiten will.
examples.english: I'm learning German because I want to work in Germany.

examples.german: Ich denke, dass Deutsch wichtig ist.
examples.english: I think that German is important.

examples.german: Sie bleibt zu Hause, weil sie krank ist.
examples.english: She stays home because she is sick.

vocabulary.exampleSentence: Ich bleibe hier, weil es regnet.
vocabulary.exampleTranslation: I'm staying here because it's raining.
vocabulary.memoryHook: Sends the verb to the end. Always a comma before 'weil'.
dass (two s) = the conjunction 'that'. Don't confuse with 'das' (the/that article).

vocabulary.exampleSentence: Ich denke, dass es gut ist.
vocabulary.memoryHook: Often introduces a dass-clause: 'Ich denke, dass …'.

vocabulary.exampleSentence: Deutsch ist wichtig für den Job.
vocabulary.exampleTranslation: German is important for the job.

vocabulary.memoryHook: Pairs with weil: '…, weil ich krank bin.'

exercises.prompt: After 'weil', where does the conjugated verb go?
options: to the end of the clause
exercises.correctAnswer: to the end of the clause
exercises.explanation: weil is a subordinating conjunction: the conjugated verb moves to the end. 'Ich lerne Deutsch, weil ich einen Job will.'

options: Ich denke, dass Deutsch ist wichtig.
Ich denke, dass Deutsch wichtig ist.
Ich denke, dass ist Deutsch wichtig.
Ich denke dass wichtig Deutsch ist.
exercises.correctAnswer: Ich denke, dass Deutsch wichtig ist.
exercises.explanation: In the dass-clause the verb 'ist' goes to the very end: 'dass Deutsch wichtig ist.' A comma always precedes 'dass'.

exercises.prompt: Complete: 'Sie bleibt zu Hause, weil sie krank ___.' (…because she is sick.)
exercises.hint: The verb goes to the end of the weil-clause.
exercises.explanation: The conjugated verb 'ist' lands at the end of the weil-clause: '…, weil sie krank ist.'

lessons.title: wenn — When & If
lessons.subtitle: Conditions and repeated events, and starting with the clause.
lessons.hook: 'When I have time, I'll call you.' 'If it rains, we'll stay home.' German uses one word — 'wenn' — for both 'when' (repeated/future) and 'if'. And like weil, it sends the verb to the end. Plus a neat twist when the sentence STARTS with the wenn-clause.
lessons.explain: 'wenn' introduces conditions ('if') and repeated/future time ('when(ever)'), and — like all subordinating conjunctions — pushes the verb to the end: 'Ich rufe dich an, wenn ich Zeit habe.' When the wenn-clause comes FIRST, the whole clause fills position 1, so the main clause's verb comes immediately after the comma (verb-comma-verb): 'Wenn ich Zeit habe, rufe ich dich an.' Note: for a single past event ('when' = as/at that time), German uses 'als' instead of 'wenn' — but for conditions and repeated events, 'wenn' is your word.

examples.german: Ich rufe dich an, wenn ich Zeit habe.
examples.english: I'll call you when I have time.

examples.german: Wenn es regnet, bleiben wir zu Hause.
examples.english: If it rains, we'll stay home.

breakdown.role: main verb right after the comma

examples.german: Wenn ich müde bin, trinke ich Kaffee.
examples.english: When(ever) I'm tired, I drink coffee.

vocabulary.exampleSentence: Wenn du willst, komme ich mit.
vocabulary.exampleTranslation: If you want, I'll come along.
vocabulary.memoryHook: Conditions + repeated events. Verb to the end. For one past event, use 'als'.
Weather verb with 'es': es regnet. In a clause: '…, wenn es regnet.'
Static verb; often in wenn-sentences about plans.

vocabulary.exampleSentence: Wenn ich Zeit habe, lese ich.
vocabulary.exampleTranslation: When I have time, I read.
vocabulary.memoryHook: 'wenn ich Zeit habe' — a phrase you'll reuse constantly.

exercises.prompt: When the wenn-clause comes first, what comes right after the comma?
exercises.explanation: The whole wenn-clause fills position 1, so the main verb follows immediately: 'Wenn es regnet, bleiben wir zu Hause.' (verb-comma-verb).

exercises.prompt: Complete: 'Ich rufe dich an, wenn ich Zeit ___.' (…when I have time.)
exercises.hint: Verb goes to the end of the wenn-clause.
exercises.explanation: The verb 'habe' moves to the end of the wenn-clause: '…, wenn ich Zeit habe.'

exercises.prompt: For a single completed past event ('when I was young'), German prefers…
exercises.explanation: Use 'als' for one specific past event ('Als ich jung war…'). 'wenn' is for conditions and repeated/future events.
